---
title: "Paper Discussion: Removing A/B Test Bias in a Marketplace of Expirable Goods"
author: "Bazyli Szymański & Alek Racicot"
date: "2026-02-21"
description: "Why A/B tests in revenue management are biased by lead time, and how to correct for it."
categories: [Revenue Management, Experimentation]
image: thumbnail.jpg
format:
  html:
    self-contained: false
bibliography: references.bib
---

Quickly why I think it's a cool paper and what we'll go through.

## It's not as simple as a t-test

A/B tests in revenue management can be quite challenging. In most settings, we
are trying to measure a very small signal (e.g. 0.5-1%) on a very noisy outcome,
and many of our products are subject to capacity constraints, cannibalization
risks, long testing horizons, mixed treatment effects (MTE), and a range of other
complications that make standard testing procedures insufficient or impractical.

Capacity constraints are central to revenue management and their considerations 
result in pricing strategies that can be significantly different from those you would
obtain pricing a fully unconstrained product. The purpose of a dynamic pricing
system is to maximise the revenue a business can generate from its inventory. In
the case of airlines or hotels, this inventory is perishable: a seat flown empty
or a room left unfilled represents lost revenue, and the business must manage its
inventory to maximise profitability before it expires.

At any point in time, an RM system prices its inventory to maximise revenue by
considering both the expected immediate reward (seats sold now at the current
price) and the expected future reward (seats it expects to sell later at a future
price). Before diving into the implications this has for our experiments let's
look at this through the lens of our example in
[Foundations of Revenue Management: From Deregulation to Littlewood's Rule](../fundamentals-of-revenue-management/index.qmd).
If you haven't read the article, here is a short problem statement:

::: {.callout-note}
Your flight has 200 seats. You sell two fares: a Saver fare at \$262 and a
Business fare at \$476. Saver demand is high enough to fill any seats not
protected for business customer who will book in the last 20 days to departure.
You expect an uncertain arrival of around 30 business customers

$$N_{\text{Business Customers}} \sim \text{Poisson}(30)$$
:::

::: {#cell-fig-revenue-by-period .cell execution_count=1}

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display}
![Revenue claimed in period 1 can't be realised in period 2](index_files/figure-html/fig-revenue-by-period-output-1.png){#fig-revenue-by-period width=1334 height=475}
:::
:::


The figure above shows the expected revenue you would generate in period 1 and
period 2 depending on the number of seats you protect. The key insight is simple:
**the seats you sell today directly determine what capacity you have left to sell
tomorrow**, and therefore what **revenue you can still generate**.

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## A sidenote on SUTVA and why we can't randomise at the booking level

This also illustrates why testing RM systems is fundamentally hard. In a standard
A/B test, we assume that exposing one customer to a treatment does not affect
other customers, which is the SUTVA assumption from the counterfactual framework. In RM, this assumption breaks immediately: every booking changes your remaining capacity, which changes your price, which affects every future customer. This is what we earlier called a mixed treatment effect. Because of this, we cannot
randomise at the individual booking level and instead randomise at a higher level,
such as the flight or the route, so that the treatment and control groups each
have their own independent inventory. Similarly, testing a new pricing engine on a
flight with a booking history will also cause issues.

![SUTVA violation in RM: a treated customer's conversion changes capacity, which changes the price seen by the next untreated customer.](sutva_violation.png)
:::

### Wait, why is my uplift metric trending?

Another way to look at this is that if we made a change to our RM system and
tracked the revenue uplift day by day, our metric would be trending. This view is
closer to what is presented in the paper [@le2023price] and is essentially what the authors
leverage to correct for the lead day bias.

To see this more explicitly, consider the figure above. If we compared a policy
of protecting 0 seats against one protecting 30 seats at the end of period 1, we
would have generated just over \$52k with the first policy and a little under
\$45k with the second. We might be tempted to conclude that the new policy
generated a 17% uplift. But by the end of period 2, the policy protecting 30
seats would have generated an additional \$11.9k from business customers, while
the policy that protected nothing would have sold out and generated zero. What
initially looked like a 17% gain would have turned into roughly an 8% loss.

@le2023price refer to this as fill rate bias. As they put it, "in A/B tests, our
revenue metric is recognised on the day of the booking regardless of the check-in
date, and it will favour the pricing strategy that sells the night at a faster
rate, but not necessarily the one that fetches the highest final price." Price
reductions far from departure look like good ideas early on, only to trend
negatively as departure approaches.
DSVKJSV
